topic: Article 19: Six Freedoms and Reasonable Restrictions
subtopic: Citizen freedoms, ground-specific restrictions, proportionality, speech, association, movement, residence and profession

content.issueStory: Freedom under Article 19 is neither absolute nor subject to a single general public-interest clause; each freedom has its own constitutionally enumerated restriction grounds. Article 19(1) guarantees six freedoms to citizens. Clauses 19(2)-19(6) permit reasonable restrictions on specified grounds. Validity requires legal authority, a listed ground for that freedom, reasonableness and increasingly proportionality in constitutional review.
content.coreConcept: Article 19(1) guarantees six freedoms to citizens. Clauses 19(2)-19(6) permit reasonable restrictions on specified grounds. Validity requires legal authority, a listed ground for that freedom, reasonableness and increasingly proportionality in constitutional review.
dimensions: Speech and expression includes communication and related derivative freedoms, subject to Article 19(2) grounds.
Peaceful assembly without arms is protected subject to Article 19(3).
Association, union or cooperative formation is protected subject to Article 19(4).
Movement and residence within India are protected subject to Article 19(5), including general-public and Scheduled Tribe interests.
Profession, occupation, trade or business is protected subject to Article 19(6), professional qualifications and State monopoly possibilities.
Restriction review examines legality, purpose, connection, necessity, balance and procedural safeguards.
content.constitutionalLink: Article 19(1)-(6); Romesh Thappar, Bennett Coleman, Shreya Singhal, Anuradha Bhasin and proportionality jurisprudence.
content.dataReport: Uploaded source basis: STATIC_09_Polity.pdf and PYQ_06_Polity_Governance.pdf. Refresh amendments, statutory rules, office status and later judgments from official sources: Constitution of India, India Code and Supreme Court judgment database.
content.caseStudy: Shreya Singhal invalidated Section 66A of the IT Act for unconstitutional vagueness and overbreadth affecting online speech, illustrating that a restriction needs more than a broadly asserted public purpose.
argumentsFor: Reasonableness balances liberty with constitutional interests.
Judicial review protects political and personal agency.
argumentsAgainst: Digital platforms complicate public-private speech power.
Broad security claims can chill expression.
Proportionality application can vary by context.
content.pyqLink: UPSC patterns test Article-institution matches, exceptions, procedure and comparative statements within Article 19: Six Freedoms and Reasonable Restrictions. Resolve each statement through source, scope, procedure and consequence.

answerFramework.intro: Introduce Article 19: Six Freedoms and Reasonable Restrictions through the controlling constitutional principle and legal source.
body: Map the authority: Article 19(1)-(6); Romesh Thappar, Bennett Coleman, Shreya Singhal, Anuradha Bhasin and proportionality jurisprudence.

Explain the first-order design: Speech and expression includes communication and related derivative freedoms, subject to Article 19(2) grounds. Peaceful assembly without arms is protected subject to Article 19(3). Association, union or cooperative formation is protected subject to Article 19(4).

Analyse safeguards and tensions: Movement and residence within India are protected subject to Article 19(5), including general-public and Scheduled Tribe interests. Profession, occupation, trade or business is protected subject to Article 19(6), professional qualifications and State monopoly possibilities. Restriction review examines legality, purpose, connection, necessity, balance and procedural safeguards.

Use Shreya Singhal invalidated Section 66A of the IT Act for unconstitutional vagueness and overbreadth affecting online speech, illustrating that a restriction needs more than a broadly asserted public purpose. and close with constitutional accountability plus dated legal verification.
answerFramework.conclusion: A strong Polity answer joins constitutional text, institutional purpose, judicial doctrine and a workable accountability safeguard.

mainsExamples: Movement restriction requiring legal and proportionate justification.
prelimsFacts: Article 19 freedoms are guaranteed to citizens.
There are six current freedoms in Article 19(1).
Speech restriction grounds are in Article 19(2).
The right to property is no longer an Article 19 freedom.
Reasonable restriction must have legal and constitutional basis.

revisionBox.prompt: What is the controlling rule in Article 19: Six Freedoms and Reasonable Restrictions?
revisionBox.answer: Article 19(1) guarantees six freedoms to citizens. Clauses 19(2)-19(6) permit reasonable restrictions on specified grounds. Validity requires legal authority, a listed ground for that freedom, reasonableness and increasingly proportionality in constitutional review.

revisionBox.prompt: What is the primary constitutional or legal authority?
revisionBox.answer: Article 19(1)-(6); Romesh Thappar, Bennett Coleman, Shreya Singhal, Anuradha Bhasin and proportionality jurisprudence.

revisionBox.prompt: What is the fastest elimination trap?
revisionBox.answer: Do not use one restriction clause for all six freedoms.

revisionBox.prompt: Where should later legal changes be verified?
revisionBox.answer: Constitution of India, India Code and Supreme Court judgment database

story: Freedom under Article 19 is neither absolute nor subject to a single general public-interest clause; each freedom has its own constitutionally enumerated restriction grounds.
core_concept: Article 19(1) guarantees six freedoms to citizens. Clauses 19(2)-19(6) permit reasonable restrictions on specified grounds. Validity requires legal authority, a listed ground for that freedom, reasonableness and increasingly proportionality in constitutional review.

mindmap_json.center: Article 19: Six Freedoms and Reasonable Restrictions

branches.label: Text
children: Speech and expression includes communication and related derivative freedoms, subject to Article 19(2) grounds.
Peaceful assembly without arms is protected subject to Article 19(3).

branches.label: Institution
children: Association, union or cooperative formation is protected subject to Article 19(4).
Movement and residence within India are protected subject to Article 19(5), including general-public and Scheduled Tribe interests.

branches.label: Safeguard
children: Profession, occupation, trade or business is protected subject to Article 19(6), professional qualifications and State monopoly possibilities.
Restriction review examines legality, purpose, connection, necessity, balance and procedural safeguards.

branches.label: Traps
children: Do not use one restriction clause for all six freedoms.
Do not say Article 19 rights belong equally to every non-citizen.
Do not treat freedom of speech as textually absolute.

key_facts: Article 19 freedoms are guaranteed to citizens.
There are six current freedoms in Article 19(1).
Speech restriction grounds are in Article 19(2).
The right to property is no longer an Article 19 freedom.
Reasonable restriction must have legal and constitutional basis.
common_traps: Do not use one restriction clause for all six freedoms.
Do not say Article 19 rights belong equally to every non-citizen.
Do not treat freedom of speech as textually absolute.
